title: Section 9: Cardiorespiratory Care – Combined MCQ (Ch 40 & 41 + Mixed)
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: The first intensive care unit (ICU) was started in which year?
questions.explanation: Page 278: First intensive care unit (ICU) was started in 1953.

questions.question: Total number of beds in ICU should be what percentage of hospital beds?
questions.explanation: Page 278: Total number of beds in ICU should be 10% of hospital beds.

questions.question: Each ICU bed should have a floor area of:
questions.explanation: Page 278: Each bed should have 20 m² floor area.

questions.question: Acute respiratory failure is considered present if pO2 is less than:
questions.explanation: Page 278: Acute respiratory failure is considered present if pO2 < 60 mm Hg, pCO2 > 50 mm Hg and pH < 7.30.

questions.question: Type I respiratory failure is characterized by:

options.A: Both pO2 and pCO2 are normal
options.B: Low pO2 with normal or low pCO2

questions.explanation: Page 278: Type I (Oxygenation Failure) is characterized by low pO2 (<60 mm Hg) with CO2 usually normal or even low due to compensatory hyperventilation.

questions.question: Type II respiratory failure is characterized by pCO2 greater than:
questions.explanation: Page 278: Type II (Hypercapnic Ventilatory Failure) is characterized by increase in pCO2 > 50 mm Hg.

questions.question: Ideally, inspired oxygen concentration (FiO2) should not exceed more than:
questions.explanation: Page 278: Ideally, FiO2 should not exceed more than 50%, otherwise oxygen toxicity can occur.

questions.question: Oxygen therapy is NOT helpful in which type of hypoxia?
questions.explanation: Page 278: Oxygen therapy is helpful in all types of hypoxia except histotoxic hypoxia.

questions.question: The usual initial tidal volume setting on a ventilator is:
questions.explanation: Page 279: Initial ventilator settings - Tidal volume: 6–8 mL/kg.

questions.question: The normal inspiratory to expiratory (I:E) ratio set on a ventilator is:
questions.explanation: Page 279: Initial ventilator setting - I:E ratio = 1:2.

questions.question: The chief advantage of volume controlled ventilation is:
questions.explanation: Page 279: The chief advantage of volume preset breath is assurance of delivery of preset tidal volume and hence decreased chances of hypoventilation.

questions.question: The chief disadvantage of pressure controlled ventilation is:
questions.explanation: Page 279: Since tidal volume varies to maintain preset pressure, the chief disadvantage of pressure controlled ventilation is increased possibility of hypoventilation.

questions.question: In controlled-mode ventilation (CMV), the major disadvantage is:

options.A: Positive intrathoracic pressure decreasing venous return and cardiac output

questions.explanation: Page 280: In CMV, intrathoracic pressure always remains positive decreasing the venous return and cardiac output.

questions.question: The most commonly used dual mode ventilation in India is:
questions.explanation: Page 281: The most commonly used dual mode in India is pressure-regulated volume control (PRVC).

questions.question: In high-frequency jet ventilation, the frequency is:
questions.explanation: Page 281: High-frequency jet ventilation has frequency of 100–300 cycles/min with gases at high pressure of 60 psi. This is the most commonly used mode for high frequency ventilation.

questions.question: The ideal PEEP is considered as the PEEP which maintains oxygen saturation >90% without decreasing cardiac output. Usually it is:
questions.explanation: Page 282: Ideal PEEP is usually 5–12 cm H₂O.

questions.question: BIPAP typical settings include inspiratory pressure (IPAP) of:
questions.explanation: Page 282: BIPAP typical settings are 8–20 cm H₂O positive pressure during inspiration and 5 cm H₂O during expiration.

questions.question: NIPPV is most frequently applied in acute setting for:
questions.explanation: Page 282: In acute setting, NIPPV is most frequently applied for acute exacerbation of COPD.

questions.question: The Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI) for weaning should be:
questions.explanation: Page 283: RSBI = Respiratory rate/Tidal volume (in liters). RSBI should be <100 for successful weaning.

questions.question: The most common source of nosocomial infections in ICU is:
questions.explanation: Page 284: Most common source of nosocomial infections in ICU are urinary tract infections accounting for 35–40% of total ICU infections.

questions.question: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) is defined as pneumonia developing in patients on ventilators for more than:
questions.explanation: Page 284: VAP is defined as pneumonia developing in patients on ventilators for >48 hours with no evidence of preintubation pneumonia.

questions.question: The simplest and most cost-effective method to decrease VAP is:

options.D: Positioning head of bed at 30 degrees

questions.explanation: Page 284: Positioning of the head of bed at 30 degrees proves to be simplest and most cost effective method to decrease VAP.

questions.question: Shock is classified into how many major types?
questions.explanation: Page 285: Shock is classified into four major types: Hypovolemic, Cardiogenic, Distributive, and Obstructive.

questions.question: In distributive shock, the hallmark finding is:
questions.explanation: Page 285: In distributive shock, poor vascular tone (decreased SVR) is the hallmark.

questions.question: In hemorrhagic shock, the target for hypotensive resuscitation is MAP of:
questions.explanation: Page 286: Current guidelines for hemorrhagic shock favor hypotensive resuscitation maintaining MAP between 60–65 mm Hg.

questions.question: The vasopressor of choice for septicemic shock is:
questions.explanation: Page 287: Noradrenaline (Norepinephrine) is the vasopressor of choice for septicemic shock.

questions.question: Phenylephrine is considered as first-line drug in which type of shock?
questions.explanation: Page 287: Phenylephrine is considered as first-line drug in neurogenic shock.

questions.question: Stroke volume variation is considered as most reliable to assess:
questions.explanation: Page 286: Stroke volume variation is considered as most reliable to assess fluid status and titrate fluid therapy.

questions.question: The vein of choice for parenteral nutrition is:
questions.explanation: Page 288: Due to large diameter (and hence less chances of thrombophlebitis), subclavian is the vein of choice for parenteral nutrition.

questions.question: Calorie requirement for a normal 70 kg man is approximately:
questions.explanation: Page 288: Calorie requirement for normal 70 kg man is 1,800 kcal/day.

questions.question: Suctioning duration in ventilated patients should not exceed:
questions.explanation: Page 288: Patient on ventilator remains apneic during suctioning therefore suctioning duration should not exceed more than 20 seconds.

questions.question: The leading cause of death in ICU is:
questions.explanation: Page 290: Sepsis is the leading cause of death in ICU.

questions.question: In septicemic shock, the colloid that should be avoided is:
questions.explanation: Page 290: Colloids especially hydroxyethyl starch (HES) should be avoided in sepsis because it may increase kidney injury.

questions.question: Pulmonary edema develops when pulmonary artery occlusion pressure rises to more than:
questions.explanation: Page 290: Pulmonary edema develops when pulmonary artery occlusion pressure rises to more than 25 mm Hg.

questions.question: According to the Berlin definition, severe ARDS is defined as PaO2/FiO2:
questions.explanation: Page 291: Berlin definition - Severe ARDS: PaO2/FiO2 ≤100 mm Hg with PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O.

questions.question: The most common cause of ARDS is:
questions.explanation: Page 291: Sepsis is the most common cause of ARDS.

questions.question: The overall mortality in ARDS is:
questions.explanation: Page 291: The overall mortality in ARDS is 30–40% and is due to multiorgan failure.

questions.question: Low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg) in ARDS has been found to decrease overall mortality by:
questions.explanation: Page 291: Low tidal volumes of 6 mL/kg have found to decrease the overall mortality significantly (9%).

questions.question: COPD patients survive on hypoxic drive, therefore oxygen supplementation should be kept at:
questions.explanation: Page 292: COPD patients survive on hypoxic drive therefore it is necessary to keep low flows (1–2 L/min).

questions.question: The affinity of carbon monoxide (CO) to bind hemoglobin compared to oxygen is:
questions.explanation: Page 292: The affinity of carbon monoxide to bind to hemoglobin is 240 times than oxygen.

questions.question: The half-life of CO at atmospheric air is approximately:
questions.explanation: Page 292: Half-life of CO at atmospheric air is 214 minutes, reduced to 1 hour with 100% oxygen and 20 minutes with hyperbaric oxygen.

questions.question: Normal arterial blood pH range is:
questions.explanation: Page 293: Normal blood gas interpretation - pH: 7.35–7.45.

questions.question: Normal pCO2 range is:
questions.explanation: Page 293: Normal blood gas - pCO2: 35–45 mm Hg.

questions.question: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation maintains pH by maintaining the ratio of bicarbonate to CO2 at:
questions.explanation: Page 293: Normal pH is maintained by the normal 20:1 ratio of bicarbonate to carbon dioxide.

questions.question: Treatment of metabolic alkalosis includes IV infusion of ammonium chloride or 0.1N HCl at a rate not more than:
questions.explanation: Page 295: IV infusion of ammonium chloride or 0.1N HCl not more than 0.2 mEq/kg/hr.

questions.question: Normal anion gap is:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Normal anion gap = 8–16 mM/L (average 12 mM/L).

questions.question: Anion gap formula is:
questions.explanation: Page 294: Anion gap = Sodium concentration – (Chloride + Bicarbonate concentration).

questions.question: Respiratory alkalosis can be caused by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Page 293: Causes of respiratory alkalosis include iatrogenic, pregnancy, salicylate poisoning, hypoxia, and CNS trauma. COPD causes respiratory acidosis.

questions.question: The most preferred crystalloid for shock resuscitation is:
questions.explanation: Page 287: Ringer is the most preferred crystalloid as it is more physiological.

questions.question: Urine output is considered as best clinical guide of:
questions.explanation: Page 286: Urine output is considered as best clinical guide of tissue perfusion.

questions.question: CPR nowadays is called as:
questions.explanation: Page 298: CPR nowadays is called as cardiopulmonary and cerebral resuscitation (CPCR).

questions.question: Unwitnessed cardiac arrest in adults should be considered due to:
questions.explanation: Page 298: Unwitnessed cardiac arrest in adults should be considered due to ventricular fibrillation until proved otherwise.

questions.question: Unwitnessed cardiac arrest in children should be considered due to:
questions.explanation: Page 298: In children, unwitnessed cardiac arrest should be considered due to asystole until proved otherwise.

questions.question: Every minute delay in starting CPR decreases prognosis by:
questions.explanation: Page 298: Every minute delay decreases prognosis by 7–10%.

questions.question: The standard sequence of CPR was changed from A-B-C to C-A-B in which year's guidelines?
questions.explanation: Page 298: The standard sequence was changed to C → A → B in 2010 CPR guidelines.

questions.question: In BLS, defibrillation is done using:
questions.explanation: Page 298: In BLS, defibrillation is done by automatic external defibrillator (AED) which can be used by lay rescuers.

questions.question: According to newer studies, the more important cause of airway obstruction in unconscious patients is:
questions.explanation: Page 299: As per newer studies, epiglottis rather than tongue fall has been found to be the more important cause of airway obstruction.

questions.question: In patients with suspected cervical spine fracture, airway should be managed by:
questions.explanation: Page 299: In cervical spine fracture, head tilt and chin lift should be avoided and airway should be managed only by jaw thrust.

questions.question: Heimlich maneuver is used for:
questions.explanation: Page 299: Heimlich maneuver - manual thrust compressing the abdomen 6–10 times. This method is used for adults and older children.

questions.question: During cardiac massage, sternum should be depressed by:

options.D: 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than 2.4 inches (6 cm)

questions.explanation: Page 300: The force should depress sternum by 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than 2.4 inches (6 cm).

questions.question: The rate of compression during cardiac massage should be:
questions.explanation: Page 301: Rate of compression should be 100–120 compressions/minute but not more than 120.

questions.question: No intervention should stop cardiac massage for more than:
questions.explanation: Page 301: No intervention (intubation, pulse check) should stop cardiac massage for >10 seconds.

questions.question: Effective cardiac output generated by successful cardiac massage is only:
questions.explanation: Page 301: Effective cardiac output generated by successful massage is only 30% of normal.

questions.question: Without advanced airway, the compression to ventilation ratio is:
questions.explanation: Page 301: Without advanced airway, the ratio should be 30:2 irrespective of number of resuscitators.

questions.question: With advanced airway in place, breathing rate during CPR is:
questions.explanation: Page 301: With advanced airway, breathing at a rate of 10 breaths/minute (1 breath every 6 seconds) without synchronization.

questions.question: Capnography during CPR: Successful cardiac massage should produce ETCO2 of at least:
questions.explanation: Page 301: Successful cardiac massage should produce ETCO2 of at least 20 mm Hg.

questions.question: ETCO2 becoming normal (40 mm Hg) is the earliest indicator of:
questions.explanation: Page 301: ETCO2 becoming normal (40 mm Hg) is the earliest indicator of return of normal circulation.

questions.question: Shockable rhythms in cardiac arrest include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Page 301: Shockable rhythms include VF, pulseless VT, and polymorphic VT (torsade de pointes). Asystole is nonshockable.

questions.question: With monophasic defibrillators, all shocks should be of:
questions.explanation: Page 302: With monophasic defibrillators all shocks should be of 360 Joules.

questions.question: Defibrillation paddle size for adults is:
questions.explanation: Page 302: Paddle size for adults: 13 cm, children: 8 cm, infants: 4.5 cm.

questions.question: The most common cause of Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) is:
questions.explanation: Page 304: Hypovolemia (shock) is the most common cause of PEA.

questions.question: The causes of PEA are divided into:
questions.explanation: Page 304: The causes of PEA have been divided into 5H and 5T.

questions.question: Targeted temperature management (TTM) after cardiac arrest means maintaining core body temperature at:
questions.explanation: Page 305: TTM means maintaining core body temperature for 32–36°C for 24 hours.

questions.question: Post cardiac arrest, moderate blood sugar should be maintained at:
questions.explanation: Page 305: Moderate blood sugar (144–180 mg/dL or 8–10 mmol) should be maintained in post-resuscitation period.

questions.question: Emergency PCI should preferably be done within how many minutes for STEMI?
questions.explanation: Page 305: Emergency (preferably within 90 minutes) coronary angiography/angioplasty is recommended for STEMI.

questions.question: In pediatric CPR, for a single rescuer, how many cycles of CPR are given before activating EMS?
questions.explanation: Page 306: For single rescuer in pediatric CPR, give 5 cycles of CPR (30:2) before going to activate EMS.

questions.question: Compression to ventilation ratio in pediatric CPR with 2 rescuers (without advanced airway) is:
questions.explanation: Page 306: For 2 rescuers the ratio is 15:2 (while for 1 rescuer it is 30:2).

questions.question: Energy selection for first shock in pediatric manual defibrillation is:
questions.explanation: Page 306: Energy selection for shock (manual) is 2 J/kg for first shock and 4 J/kg subsequently.

questions.question: In newborn CPR, the compression to ventilation ratio is:
questions.explanation: Page 306: Newborn CPR compression ventilation ratio is 3:1 (90 compressions with 30 breaths) and synchronized.

questions.question: Indication to start chest compressions in newborn is HR less than:
questions.explanation: Page 306: Indication of chest compression in newborn is HR < 60/min in spite of adequate ventilation with 100% oxygen for 30 seconds.

questions.question: In CPR during pregnancy, aortocaval compression should be achieved by:
questions.explanation: Page 307: Lateral tilt has been eliminated from new CPR guidelines and aortocaval compression should be achieved by uterine displacement.

questions.question: Emergency LSCS during CPR should be considered if fetus is >25 weeks and can be performed within:
questions.explanation: Page 307: Emergency LSCS should be considered if fetus >25 weeks and LSCS can be performed within 4 minutes of maternal arrest.

questions.question: The fluid of choice during CPR is:
questions.explanation: Page 307: Glucose containing solutions and Ringer lactate should be avoided during CPR. Fluid of choice is normal saline.

questions.question: Drugs that can be delivered by endotracheal route include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Page 307: Drugs deliverable by ET route are Adrenaline, Atropine, Lignocaine, and Naloxone. Amiodarone is not on this list.

questions.question: The dose by endotracheal route is how many times of IV dose?
questions.explanation: Page 307: The dose by endotracheal route is 2.5 times of intravenous dose.

questions.question: Intracardiac adrenaline in present day CPR is:
questions.explanation: Page 308: Adrenaline by intracardiac route is contraindicated in present day CPR as injection in myocardium can cause significant myocardial injury.

questions.question: Routine use of sodium bicarbonate in CPR is NOT recommended because:

options.B: It produces CO2 which can worsen neuronal injury

questions.explanation: Page 308: Sodium bicarbonate on metabolism produces CO2 which can change cerebral pH and worsen neuronal injury.

questions.question: Sodium bicarbonate in CPR should only be given if pH is less than:
questions.explanation: Page 308: Sodium bicarbonate should be given only if there is proven acidosis (pH <7.15).

questions.question: The only recommended modality for global cerebral ischemia in CPR is:
questions.explanation: Page 308: The only recommended modality for global cerebral ischemia seen in CPR is targeted temperature management.

questions.question: Patient can be declared dead only after resuscitation for how many minutes?
questions.explanation: Page 308: Patient can be declared dead only after resuscitation of 20 minutes.

questions.question: Brain death declaration should be done by how many doctors?
questions.explanation: Page 308: Declaration should be done by two doctors; one among those should be the neurologist.

questions.question: Amiodarone is considered drug of choice for:
questions.explanation: Page 309: Amiodarone is considered as drug of choice for ventricular tachycardia. First dose is 300 mg IV.

questions.question: As per 2015 guidelines, vasopressin in CPR is:

options.B: Given after first dose of adrenaline
options.D: Given after second dose of adrenaline

questions.explanation: Page 309, Table 41.2: Vasopressin has been removed from 2015 CPR guidelines.

questions.question: Prognostication after cardiac arrest should be done at least how many hours after ROSC?
questions.explanation: Page 305: Prognostication is recommended 72 hours after return of spontaneous circulation.

questions.question: Intraosseous route for drug delivery in CPR is recommended for:
questions.explanation: Page 307: As per current guidelines, intraosseous route is recommended for all ages.

questions.question: Subclavian vein is the most preferred central vein in emergency because:
questions.explanation: Page 307: Subclavian is the most preferred central vein in emergency situations.

questions.question: Survival is better if BLS is initiated within how many minutes of arrest?
questions.explanation: Page 307: Survival is better if BLS is initiated within 4 minutes of arrest, ALS within 8 minutes.

questions.question: The most common cause of cardiac arrest (which has 50-60% success rate if detected in time) is:
questions.explanation: Page 307: Most common cause of cardiac arrest is ventricular fibrillation which if detected in time can have 50 to 60% success rate.

questions.question: Calcium during CPR is recommended ONLY for:

options.B: Hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, and calcium channel blocker overdose

questions.explanation: Page 308: Calcium is only recommended if arrest is due to hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia, and overdose of calcium channel blockers.

questions.question: As per 2015 CPR guidelines, chest compression rate upper limit is:
questions.explanation: Page 309, Table 41.2: 2015 guidelines state compression rate is 100–120 but not more than 120 (2010 had no upper limit).

questions.question: Why is nitrous oxide contraindicated for tympanoplasties?

options.A: Increase in middle ear pressure can displace the graft

questions.explanation: Increase middle ear pressure by nitrous oxide can displace the graft making nitrous oxide to be contraindicated for Tympanoplasties.

questions.question: Aspiration of late pregnancy was described by Mendelson and is called:
questions.explanation: Page 137: Pregnancy (acid aspiration in late pregnancy) was described by Mendelson and is called as Mendelson syndrome. The gravid uterus compresses the stomach leading to distortion of gastro-esophageal angle making LES to become incompetent.

questions.question: According to the Meyer Overton rule, the anesthetic potency of an inhalational agent is directly proportional to its:
questions.explanation: Page 89: Anesthetic potency of an inhalational agent is directly proportional to its lipid solubility (oil gas partition coefficient). This is called the Meyer Overton rule, which led to the unitary theory of narcosis.

questions.question: Which form of obesity is stated as a BARRIER to day-care procedures according to the textbook?

options.A: Obesity in ASA grade II patients
options.C: Morbid obesity with obstructive sleep apnea

questions.explanation: Page 267: Obesity (not even the morbid obesity with obstructive sleep apnea) is a barrier to day-care procedures. This means simple obesity alone is NOT a barrier; only morbid obesity combined with obstructive sleep apnea is considered a barrier to day-care surgery.
questions.topic: Mixed Review – Selection of Surgery and Patients

questions.question: After a cerebrovascular accident, elective surgery should be deferred for:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. 9 months — After cerebrovascular accident, elective surgery should be deferred for 9 months. The odds are stabilized at 3 times after 9 months.

questions.question: Which anesthetic agent should be AVOIDED in Parkinson's disease due to its antidopaminergic effects?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Droperidol — Droperidol and other butyrophenones have antidopaminergic activity and can worsen parkinsonian symptoms. Metoclopramide should also be avoided for the same reason.

questions.question: Why is premedication with benzodiazepines avoided in patients with pulmonary disease?

options.B: These patients are more sensitive to respiratory depression

questions.explanation: The text states 'Premedication with benzodiazepines should be avoided as these patients are more sensitive to respiratory depression.'

questions.question: A stimulated nerve is blocked earlier than a nonstimulated nerve. This kind of block is called:
questions.explanation: Page 153: Since activated channels are blocked more easily, a stimulated nerve will be blocked earlier than a nonstimulated nerve. This kind of block by local anesthetics is called use-dependent block.

questions.question: According to the home readiness criteria, for how long must a patient's vital signs be stable before discharge from day-care surgery?
questions.explanation: Page 269: Criteria for home readiness include: (i) Vital signs are stable for more than 60 minutes. This ensures that the patient has had adequate time for hemodynamic stabilization after the procedure before being discharged.

questions.question: In myasthenia gravis, the response to muscle relaxants is:

options.C: Resistance to depolarizers and increased sensitivity to nondepolarizers
options.D: Increased sensitivity to depolarizers and resistance to nondepolarizers

questions.explanation: Page 131, Table 13.2: In myasthenia gravis there is resistance to depolarizers and increased sensitivity to nondepolarizers. This autoimmune disease destroys acetylcholine receptors, leaving fewer receptors for succinylcholine (increasing resistance) but less competition for nondepolarizers (increasing their sensitivity).

questions.question: What is the classic triad of fat embolism syndrome?
questions.explanation: The triad of fat embolism syndrome is dyspnea, confusion and petechial hemorrhages.

questions.question: What is the MOST COMMON cause of RE-ADMISSION after day-care surgery according to the textbook?

options.B: Surgical complications such as surgical site infection

questions.explanation: Page 269: Most often the re-admission is due to surgical complications, such as surgical site infection. This is highlighted in the Key Points section. Note the distinction: while uncontrolled pain and PONV are the most common causes of INABILITY TO DISCHARGE, surgical complications are the most common cause of RE-ADMISSION.

questions.question: What is the clinical significance of decreased pseudocholinesterase levels in geriatric patients?

options.A: Prolongs the effect of suxamethonium clinically
options.B: Requires avoidance of all muscle relaxants
options.C: Theoretically prolongs suxamethonium effect but clinically no prolongation is seen
options.D: Has no effect on any drug

questions.explanation: Page 243: Decreased pseudocholinesterase levels due to decreased hepatic functions may theoretically prolong the effect of suxamethonium, however clinically no prolongation has been seen.
questions.topic: Mixed Review – Anesthetic Management - General Anesthesia

questions.question: Which muscle is considered the most resistant to muscle relaxants?
questions.explanation: Page 123: Laryngeal muscles and diaphragm may require more than double the doses required to block adductor pollicis. In fact, the diaphragm is considered as the most resistant muscle.
questions.topic: Mixed Review – Sequence of Muscle Blockade

questions.question: Should chest tube drainage be used routinely for capnothorax?

options.A: No, reserved only for cases not responding to conservative measures or life-threatening ones

questions.explanation: Page 249: Chest tube drainage should be reserved only for the cases not responding to conservative measures or the Pneumothorax is life-threatening.

questions.question: Patients with atlantoaxial subluxation cannot tolerate which neck movement?
questions.explanation: Patients of atlantoaxial subluxation cannot tolerate flexion as it can cause odontoid process to compress spinal cord.
questions.topic: Mixed Review – Rheumatoid Arthritis – Atlantoaxial Instability

questions.question: Diffusion hypoxia (Fink effect) at the end of surgery is prevented by:

options.C: Giving 100% oxygen for 5–10 minutes after discontinuing nitrous oxide

questions.explanation: Page 92: Towards the end of surgery when nitrous oxide delivery is stopped, the gradient reverses and nitrous oxide gushes into alveoli replacing oxygen, causing hypoxia (diffusion hypoxia). To avoid this, 100% oxygen should be given for 5–10 minutes after discontinuing nitrous oxide.

questions.question: According to Table 10.2, which agent has the LOWEST blood gas partition coefficient (0.14), giving the fastest induction?
questions.explanation: Page 91, Table 10.2: Xenon has the lowest blood gas partition coefficient (0.14), so the fastest induction is seen with xenon. Among the agents used now-a-day, induction is fastest with desflurane (0.42) and slowest with halothane (2.4).

questions.question: The onset of action of a nondepolarizer is inversely proportional to its:
questions.explanation: Page 127–128: Onset of a nondepolarizer is inversely proportional to potency. More the potency, lesser the dose required, means less molecules delivered at the neuromuscular junction to compete with acetylcholine, delaying the onset of action.

questions.question: Before surgery, CIED should be programmed to asynchronous mode. In this mode, the pacemaker delivers a fixed rate of approximately:
questions.explanation: In asynchronous mode, pacemakers deliver a fixed rate of 70–72 beats/minute irrespective of the patient's own rate, avoiding the risk of CIED being affected by interference.
questions.topic: Mixed Review – Cardiac Implanted Electronic Devices

questions.question: Ketamine in a patient taking theophylline can precipitate:
questions.explanation: The text states 'Ketamine in patient taking theophylline can precipitate seizures.'

questions.question: During laparoscopic surgery in pregnancy, what should the surgeon ensure?

options.B: Use of minimum intra-abdominal pressure to prevent hypotension
options.D: Use of general anesthesia only without monitoring

questions.explanation: Page 234: During laparoscopies, ensure that the surgeon uses minimum intra-abdominal pressure to prevent hypotension.
questions.topic: Mixed Review – Non-Obstetric Surgeries During Pregnancy

questions.question: Why does CO2 limit the use of central neuraxial blocks for laparoscopy?

options.C: Due to diaphragmatic irritation by CO2 causing patient discomfort (shoulder pain)

questions.explanation: Page 250: Patient discomfort (shoulder pain due to diaphragmatic irritation by CO2) limits the use of central neuraxial blocks (spinal/epidural) for laparoscopy.

questions.question: Potency of an inhalational agent is best determined by which parameter?
questions.explanation: Page 90: The best estimate for determining the potency of an inhalational anesthetic is minimum alveolar concentration (MAC), defined as the minimum concentration of agent to produce immobility in 50% of subjects given noxious stimuli.

questions.question: How much does cardiac output increase per kg of fat to perfuse extra fat?
questions.explanation: Increase in cardiac output by 0.1 L/min to perfuse each kg of fat leads to increased stroke volume and hence left ventricular hypertrophy.

questions.question: A patient with IHD and normal LV function is undergoing surgery. Which of the following should be AVOIDED during maintenance of anesthesia?
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide should be avoided in IHD patients for two reasons: it may cause mild sympathetic stimulation and it is a pulmonary vasoconstrictor. Inhalational agents can be safely used if LV function is normal.

questions.question: Why must oxygen cylinders in MRI suites be made of aluminum?

options.D: They must be MRI compatible (nonferrous) - ferrous materials are attracted by the magnetic field

questions.explanation: For MRI suites, everything including oxygen cylinders must be MRI compatible, i.e. nonferrous. The equipments used in MRI suites are made up of aluminum.

questions.question: How should a trauma patient always be considered regarding stomach contents?

options.D: As having a full stomach until proved otherwise, managed like rapid sequence induction

questions.explanation: A trauma patient should always be considered full stomach until proved otherwise and should be managed like rapid sequence induction.

questions.question: What should the endotracheal tube cuff be filled with in hyperbaric chamber?
questions.explanation: Endotracheal tube cuff should be filled with water or saline.

questions.question: What conduction abnormality is common in ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation: Bundle branch block is common in ankylosing spondylitis.

questions.question: What is the ideal duration for stopping smoking before surgery?
questions.explanation: The text states 'Ideally smoking should be stopped 8 weeks before surgery however stopping smoking 12 hours before may be beneficial by reducing carboxyhemoglobin levels.'

questions.question: What is the danger of administering methylene blue to G6PD deficient patients?
questions.explanation: Administrating methylene blue to these patients can be life threatening.

questions.question: How can carbon dioxide from peritoneal cavity cause pneumothorax?

options.B: Through embryonic remnants between pleural and peritoneal cavity

questions.explanation: Page 249: Carbon dioxide from peritoneal cavity can diffuse into the pleural cavity through embryonic remnants present between pleural and peritoneal cavity which gets open by increased intra-abdominal pressure or through pleural tears during surgery.

questions.question: Chronic headache patients are prone to develop post-spinal headache, therefore:

options.A: Prophylactic blood patch should be given

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Spinal should be avoided — These patients are prone to develop post-spinal headache therefore, spinal should be avoided.

questions.question: The superior laryngeal nerve block anesthetizes the airway:

options.D: From the epiglottis to the vocal cords (supraglottic area)

questions.explanation: The internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve supplies sensation from the epiglottis to and including the vocal cords.

questions.question: The first successful demonstration of ether anesthesia by Morton took place at which hospital (Fig. 8.1)?
questions.explanation: Page 83, Fig. 8.1: The first successful demonstration of ether anesthesia was by Morton in Massachusetts General Hospital.

questions.question: Which cells of the alveolar epithelium secrete surfactant?
questions.explanation: Page 4: The alveolar epithelium has type I and type II cells. Type II cells secrete surfactant.

questions.question: Which lumbar block is used to provide analgesia for hip and knee surgery?
questions.explanation: Page 271: Lumbar plexus block (also known as psoas compartment block) is given to provide analgesia for hip and knee surgery. Femoral nerve block and fascia iliaca (modified femoral) block are utilized for hip fractures and hip surgeries.

questions.question: Which type of laryngoscope blade is preferred for difficult intubation?
questions.explanation: The McCoy blade has a hinged tip that can be levered to lift the epiglottis, improving the view in difficult laryngoscopy.

questions.question: Brachial plexus block is the second most commonly performed block. Which is the most commonly performed block?
questions.explanation: Page 160: Brachial plexus block is the second most commonly performed block (after central neuraxial block). It is used for surgery of upper limb and shoulder, and can be blocked by 4 approaches — interscalene, supraclavicular, infraclavicular and axillary.

questions.question: Why do Venturi type devices deliver higher oxygen at high altitude?

options.D: Because of decreased density and resistance

questions.explanation: Venturi type devices deliver higher oxygen (because of decreased density and resistance).

questions.question: What is HAART in the context of HIV management?
questions.explanation: HAART stands for Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy used in HIV management.

questions.question: Universal precautions intraoperatively in hepatic patients must be taken if the patient is positive for:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hepatitis B or C — Universal precautions must be taken if the patient is positive for hepatitis B or C to protect the operating room staff.

questions.question: Which agent has the property of being the longest acting, most potent, and most toxic local anesthetic?
questions.explanation: Page 157, Table 15.2: Dibucaine (cinchocaine) is longest acting, most potent and most toxic local anesthetic.

questions.question: Why is pancuronium contraindicated in HCM?

options.A: It has a prolonged duration of action
options.B: It can cause sympathetic stimulation which worsens LVOT obstruction

questions.explanation: Pancuronium is contraindicated in HCM because it can cause sympathetic stimulation, which increases myocardial contractility and worsens LVOT obstruction.

questions.question: Which adjuvant therapy for cancer pain is a directly acting calcium channel blocker?
questions.explanation: Page 273: Adjuvant therapies for cancer pain like antidepressants, pregabalin/gabapentin and ziconotide (directly acting calcium channel blocker) are used. For intractable pain, surgical intervention (cordotomy) should be considered.

questions.question: In Child-Pugh classification, ascites in Group A is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. None — Per Table 21.1: Group A: No ascites, Group B: Moderate, Group C: Marked.

questions.question: Atypical pseudocholinesterase is a genetic disorder diagnosed by which test?
questions.explanation: Page 126: Atypical (abnormal) pseudocholinesterase is a genetic disease in which the patient has abnormal enzyme not able to metabolize suxamethonium. Incidence is 1 in 3,000. This is diagnosed by the dibucaine number. Dibucaine inhibits 80% of normal enzyme and 20% of abnormal enzyme.

questions.question: Which of the following are associated conditions with hypothyroidism that should be ruled out preoperatively?

options.C: Congestive cardiac failure, anemia, hypothermia, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia

questions.explanation: Associated conditions like CCF, anemia, hypothermia, hyponatremia, and hypoglycemia should be ruled out.

questions.question: Atracurium undergoes a unique method of degradation which is pH and temperature dependent. What is this called?
questions.explanation: Page 129: The majority of atracurium undergoes spontaneous degradation in plasma called Hoffman degradation. Hoffman degradation is a pH and temperature-dependent reaction; higher pH and temperature favor more degradation.
